title: Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition
Identify key diagnostic features of Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition
Apply clinical reasoning to Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition management
Recognize common pitfalls in Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition

sections.title: Overview of Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition
sections.content:
**Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition**

Phases of wound healing, primary vs secondary vs tertiary intention, factors impairing healing

**Key Points:**
• Phases: Hemostasis (minutes) → Inflammation (days 1-4) → Proliferation (days 4-21, collagen deposition) → Remodeling (weeks-months)
• Primary intention: clean wound edges approximated (surgical closure)
• Secondary intention: wound left open to granulate (contaminated/infected wounds)

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Phases: Hemostasis (minutes) → Inflammation (days 1-4) → Proliferation (days 4-21, collagen deposition) → Remodeling (weeks-months)
• Primary intention: clean wound edges approximated (surgical closure)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Secondary intention: wound left open to granulate (contaminated/infected wounds)
• Tertiary (delayed primary): wound left open initially, closed after infection controlled
• Impaired healing: diabetes, malnutrition (vitamin C, zinc, protein), steroids, radiation, infection, ischemia

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition:**

• Secondary intention: wound left open to granulate (contaminated/infected wounds)
• Tertiary (delayed primary): wound left open initially, closed after infection controlled
• Impaired healing: diabetes, malnutrition (vitamin C, zinc, protein), steroids, radiation, infection, ischemia

options.text: Phases: Hemostasis (minutes) → Inflammation (days 1-4) → Proliferation (days 4-21, collagen deposition) → Remodeling (weeks-months)
options.explanation: Phases: Hemostasis (minutes) → Inflammation (days 1-4) → Proliferation (days 4-21, collagen deposition) → Remodeling (weeks-months)

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

options.explanation: Impaired healing: diabetes, malnutrition (vitamin C, zinc, protein), steroids, radiation, infection, ischemia

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Wound Healing & Surgical Nutrition. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Primary intention: clean wound edges approximated (surgical closure)
options.explanation: Primary intention: clean wound edges approximated (surgical closure)

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.
